business_goals: Minimize systems acquisition and sustainment costs
Reduce the total ownership cost over the life of the avionics system
Modernize the system with minimal impact to Army Special Operations readiness
Ensure that new CAASs work well with other programs
technical_constraints: Large set of applicable standards (ARINC 661, POSIX, CORBA, IEEE P1386/P1386.1, OpenGL, DO 178B)
Must support multiple helicopter platforms (A/MH-6, MH-47, MH-60)

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The CAAS was already well into development at the time of evaluation. Evaluators performed a pre-exercise review of architecture documentation to capture approaches.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How does the cyclic executive ensure real-time performance?
How is the system partitioned to ensure availability and safety?

hardware_view_description: Hardware is portable between platforms ('plug and play' architecture).
Applications are location-transparent and can migrate to other hardware for load balancing.
Every application is resident on every box.
runtime_constraints: Inviolable timing and memory walls between applications (POSIX partitioning).
Applications must not overrun processing time limits or memory allotments.

guaranteed_resources.sockets: Number of sockets is known and guaranteed

questions: Does the partitioning strategy successfully isolate failures?
Do layers effectively isolate high-level graphics services?
Does the client-server approach effectively decouple system parts?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Invalid data is entered by the pilot, and the system does not crash.
Invalid data comes from an actor on any bus, and the system does not crash.
When a 1.9-second power interruption occurs, the system will execute a warm boot and be fully operational in 2 seconds.
A loss of Doppler occurs, the pilot is notified, and the Doppler timer begins a countdown.
A partition fails, the rest of the processor continues working, and the system continues to function.
The data concentrator suffers battle damage, and all flight-critical information is still available.
The mission processor in the outboard MFD fails, and that display and the rest of the system continue to operate normally.

architectural_approaches.description: The system employs a layered approach using POSIX-compliant interfaces to decouple application software from the underlying hardware and Operating System, facilitating portability across different airframes.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Enables the migration of avionics functions between different helicopter platforms (A/MH-6, MH-47, MH-60) without rewriting core logic.
Reduces lifecycle costs by allowing hardware upgrades or OS changes with minimal impact on application software.

architectural_approaches.description: Uses a Common Object Request Broker Architecture (CORBA) to handle inter-process communication and location transparency for distributed components.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Components can be relocated or updated independently as long as the CORBA interfaces remain consistent.
Standardized middleware provides a robust communication framework for critical avionics data exchange.

architectural_approaches.description: Adopts the ARINC 661 standard to separate the definition of the User Interface (the GUI) from the underlying application logic (the User Application).

addressed_attributes.rationale: Allows for changes in cockpit display layouts and user interactions without modifying the functional flight code.
Standardizes display management across platforms, reducing the need for unique display software for each helicopter type.

architectural_approaches.description: Organizes system functionality into modular, reusable components that can be shared across the Army Special Operations helicopter fleet.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Promotes the use of proven, tested components across multiple platforms, reducing the risk of new defects.
Enables optimization of specific functional components while maintaining overall system integrity.

children.scenario_description: Porting an existing avionics function from MH-60 to A/MH-6 requires < 10% change to core application logic.
ARINC 661 cockpit display must reflect pilot input within 100ms under normal flight operations.
If a CORBA broker instance fails, the system must switch to a redundant broker within 500ms without loss of flight-critical data.
Upgrading the POSIX-compliant OS to a newer version requires < 2 person-months of integration and testing effort.

scenarios.scenario_text: Porting an existing avionics function from MH-60 to A/MH-6 requires < 10% change to core application logic.
ARINC 661 cockpit display must reflect pilot input within 100ms under normal flight operations.
If a CORBA broker instance fails, the system must switch to a redundant broker within 500ms without loss of flight-critical data.
Upgrading the POSIX-compliant OS to a newer version requires < 2 person-months of integration and testing effort.

scenario_reference.text: Porting an existing avionics function from MH-60 to A/MH-6 requires < 10% change to core application logic.

analysis_records.stimulus: Request to port software to a different helicopter platform with different hardware
analysis_records.response_measure: Code changes restricted to < 10% of core application logic
analysis_records.reasoning: By isolating hardware-specific logic within a HAL and using POSIX for OS services, the core application remains platform-agnostic, minimizing the impact of hardware changes.

risks.rationale: If developers use non-standard POSIX extensions provided by a specific OS vendor, the portability goal will be compromised.

non_risks.rationale: The use of well-defined HAL interfaces ensures that hardware swaps only require driver-level updates.

scenario_reference.text: ARINC 661 cockpit display must reflect pilot input within 100ms under normal flight operations.

analysis_records.stimulus: Pilot interacts with a cockpit control (e.g., button press or dial turn)
architectural_decisions: CORBA Middleware for User Application (UA) to Cockpit Display System (CDS) communication
analysis_records.reasoning: ARINC 661 decouples UI from logic, but the performance depends on the efficiency of the CORBA bus and the CDS rendering engine.

risks.rationale: High-frequency display updates may be delayed by the serialization/deserialization overhead of CORBA messages.

non_risks.rationale: Using standardized widgets reduces the amount of data that needs to be transmitted compared to sending raw pixel data.

tradeoff_points.parameter: Display Refresh Rate vs. Bus Load

scenario_reference.text: If a CORBA broker instance fails, the system must switch to a redundant broker within 500ms without loss of flight-critical data.

analysis_records.stimulus: Primary CORBA broker process crash or hardware failure
analysis_records.reasoning: Redundancy and location transparency provided by the Broker pattern allow for failover, provided the detection mechanism is fast enough.

risks.rationale: Complex election algorithms or state synchronization between brokers can lead to 'split-brain' scenarios or exceed the 500ms window.

non_risks.rationale: CORBA's built-in support for re-binding to object references simplifies client-side recovery logic.

scenario_reference.text: Upgrading the POSIX-compliant OS to a newer version requires < 2 person-months of integration and testing effort.

analysis_records.stimulus: OS vendor releases a mandatory security/performance patch or version upgrade
architectural_decisions: Strict adherence to POSIX PSE52/PSE54 profiles
analysis_records.reasoning: Strict adherence to standardized profiles minimizes the surface area of changes required when the underlying OS kernel is updated.

risks.rationale: If the system depends on libraries that are not POSIX-compliant, upgrading the OS may break those dependencies, increasing effort.

non_risks.rationale: Existing automated tests for the application layer significantly reduce the manual effort required for OS validation.
